title: The trigger of someone withdrawing or going silent
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The moment the line goes quiet

You send a text. Something normal. A question, a check-in, a vulnerable share. Then you wait. And wait. The little dots that mean they're typing never appear. An hour passes. Then a day. Then you start checking your phone obsessively, refreshing, wondering if your message somehow got lost in the digital void. But it didn't. They just went silent.

Here's the thing. For someone with a history of relational trauma, that silence isn't just silence. It's a physical sensation. A drop in the stomach. A tightness in the chest. A voice that whispers, "You did something wrong. You're too much. They're leaving."

I know this because I've lived it. I've sat staring at a screen, my nervous system screaming at me that I was about to be abandoned, while the rational part of my brain tried to remind me that people get busy, that phones die, that not everyone checks their messages every five minutes. But the rational part of my brain doesn't run the show when a trigger hits. The survival brain does.

And survival brain has a very specific response to withdrawal and silence. It's not just emotional. It's biological. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would tell you that your nervous system is scanning for safety and danger at all times, below your conscious awareness. When someone you're attached to goes silent, your nervous system detects a potential threat to connection. And connection, for mammals like us, is a survival need.

So your body does what it's wired to do. It mobilizes. It panics. It sends out distress signals. You might feel the urge to text again. To call. To demand an answer. Or you might freeze, going numb, telling yourself you don't care anyway. Both are trauma responses. Both make perfect sense given what your system has learned about silence.

What silence meant for you then

If you grew up with caregivers who used withdrawal as punishment, or who were unpredictable in their availability, your nervous system learned a specific equation. Silence equals danger. Withdrawal equals abandonment. This wasn't a thought you had. It was a pattern your body recorded, stored in implicit memory, ready to activate at the slightest hint of similar conditions.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma is held in the body, not in the story we tell about it. So when someone withdraws now, your body isn't responding to the present moment. It's responding to every time you were left alone as a child, every time you cried and no one came, every time you reached out and found empty space where connection should have been.

That's why the reaction can feel so outsized. So disproportionate to the actual situation. Because it's not disproportionate to what your system has stored. It's a perfect match for an old wound that never healed. Think about it. If a person was burned by fire as a child, they don't need a full inferno to react. A candle flame will do. The same is true for relational withdrawal. A few hours of silence can feel like a lifetime of abandonment.

I've had clients tell me they feel crazy for how much they react to someone not texting back. They know intellectually that it's not a big deal. They can see the evidence that the person is trustworthy. But the feeling doesn't care about evidence. The feeling is older than logic. The feeling is a child standing in an empty hallway, calling out for someone who doesn't come.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation helps explain why parts of us can be triggered into a child state while other parts watch, embarrassed and confused. That book helped me understand why I could be both the panicked child and the annoyed adult at the same time, fighting over the same phone.

The two faces of the withdrawal trigger

There are actually two distinct ways this trigger shows up, and they look almost opposite. The first is protest behavior. This is the urge to chase, to demand, to get an answer. You send more texts. You leave a voicemail. You show up at their door. Your nervous system is in sympathetic activation, the fight response, trying to restore connection by force. It's desperate and it's loud and it often pushes people further away, which confirms your worst fear.

The second is shutdown. This is the freeze response. You go numb. You tell yourself you don't care. You withdraw first, before they can withdraw from you. You stop reaching out entirely. You convince yourself that you're fine alone, that you don't need anyone, that relationships are too painful to bother with. This looks calm on the outside but inside your system is collapsed, immobilized, dissociating from the pain of disconnection.

Both of these responses are trying to protect you. The protest is trying to restore connection. The shutdown is trying to prevent further injury. Neither is wrong. Neither is bad. They're survival strategies that kept you alive in an environment where connection was unreliable. The problem is they don't work well in adult relationships where silence might mean something completely different.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote extensively on how trauma reshapes the brain and body, would say that these responses are imprints of the past living in the present. The body doesn't know that time has passed. It doesn't know that the person on the other end of the silence is not the same as the caregiver who disappeared. It just knows the pattern. Silence. Danger. Activate.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk's research makes it clear that you can't talk your way out of a nervous system response. You have to work with the body directly.

What actually helps when the silence hits

So what do you do when you feel that drop, that panic, that urge to send another text or to delete the conversation and pretend you don't care? The first thing is to recognize what's happening. Say it out loud. "I am being triggered by silence. This feeling is from my past, not just my present." That alone can create a tiny space between the trigger and your reaction.

Then you need to regulate your nervous system. Not by thinking. By doing. Stand up and shake your hands. Press your feet into the floor. Take a breath and make your exhale longer than your inhale. A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It forces that extended exhale that activates the vagus nerve and signals safety to your system.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you tense up reading that? Did you feel a little relief at the idea of having something concrete to do? That's your system responding to the possibility of regulation. It's there. It's just been overwhelmed.

Here's another thing. You can't control whether someone responds to you. You can't control whether they withdraw or go silent. But you can control what you do with your own nervous system in the meantime. That's not victim blaming. That's empowerment in the only place it actually exists, which is inside your own body. Right?

I know it's hard. I know the urge to check, to chase, to collapse, is overwhelming. But every time you ride the wave of that trigger without acting on it, you're teaching your nervous system a new pattern. You're showing it that silence doesn't always mean abandonment. That you can survive the discomfort. That the person might come back. And even if they don't, you'll still be here, breathing, alive, whole.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). Lindsay Gibson's work helps explain why withdrawal felt so dangerous in the first place, because it often meant being left with a parent who couldn't attune to you, who couldn't handle your emotions, who disappeared when you needed them most.

The silence you can make peace with

I'm not going to tell you that you'll stop being triggered by withdrawal entirely. That's not how healing works. Triggers don't disappear. They just get quieter. They lose their grip. You learn to notice them without being controlled by them.

But there's another layer here. Sometimes the silence isn't about the other person at all. Sometimes it's about the silence you carry inside yourself. The parts of you that went quiet because it wasn't safe to speak. The parts that learned to withdraw before anyone else could. The parts that still believe, somewhere deep, that your needs are too much, that your voice is a burden, that silence is safer than being rejected for asking for what you want.

Dan Siegel, who developed interpersonal neurobiology, talks about how we internalize our attachment patterns. The way we were treated becomes the way we treat ourselves. If you were left in silence, you learned to leave yourself in silence. You stopped asking your own heart what it needed because you assumed no one would answer.

So maybe the trigger isn't just about them. Maybe it's also about the silence you've been keeping with yourself. The conversations you haven't had. The feelings you haven't voiced. The parts of you that have been waiting, patiently, for you to turn around and say, "I see you. I hear you. I'm not going anywhere."

That's the silence that can heal. Not the silence of abandonment, but the silence of listening. The silence where you finally stop running from your own experience and just sit with it. Let it speak. Let it be heard.

Stay with me here. This is the hard part. Because it means you have to be the one who shows up for yourself, even when no one else does. It means you have to break the pattern of withdrawal by withdrawing your withdrawal. By staying present. By refusing to abandon yourself the way you were abandoned.

It's not easy. But it's possible. And it starts with one breath. One moment where you choose to stay instead of run. One text you don't send because you know, deep down, that your worth isn't determined by whether someone answers.

The trigger of someone withdrawing or going silent is real. It's painful. It's rooted in history that you didn't choose. But you can choose what happens next. You can choose to meet yourself in the silence instead of losing yourself in it.

That's the work. That's the healing. And you're already doing it, just by reading this, just by staying with this discomfort instead of looking away.

Keep going.
